questions.text: What does 'become: true' do in an Ansible play?

options.text: Escalates privileges (e.g., sudo) on managed nodes

questions.explanation: 'become: true' enables privilege escalation, which by default uses sudo to run tasks as root. You can customize it with become_user, become_method, and become_flags. It replaces the deprecated 'sudo: yes' directive.

questions.text: When does a handler run in Ansible?

options.text: After every task in the play
Only when explicitly called with ansible.builtin.meta
Once at the end of a play, only if notified by a changed task

questions.explanation: Handlers run once at the end of a play, and only if at least one task that notified them reported a 'changed' status. If multiple tasks notify the same handler, it still only runs once. Use 'meta: flush_handlers' to run handlers mid-play.

options.text: System properties automatically gathered from managed nodes
Encrypted variables stored in Ansible Vault

questions.explanation: Facts are variables automatically gathered from managed nodes during the 'Gathering Facts' task. They include OS type, IP addresses, memory, CPU info, etc. Available via ansible_facts['distribution'], etc. You can disable gathering with 'gather_facts: false'.

citations.label: Discovering variables: facts and magic variables

questions.text: Which module should you use to install packages in a cross-platform way (works on both apt and yum)?
questions.explanation: ansible.builtin.package is a generic OS package manager module that delegates to the appropriate package manager (apt, yum, dnf, etc.) based on the OS. Use it for cross-platform playbooks. Use apt/yum directly when you need platform-specific options.

questions.text: How do you reference a variable named 'http_port' in a task?
questions.explanation: Ansible uses Jinja2 templating syntax: {{ variable_name }}. Always quote strings that start with {{ in YAML to avoid parsing errors: e.g., dest: "{{ install_dir }}/config.conf". Double braces are mandatory — single braces are not valid.

questions.text: What does the 'when' clause do in an Ansible task?

options.text: Sets a timeout for the task
Skips the task unless the condition is true
Waits for a condition before starting the task
Runs the task on a specific schedule

questions.explanation: The 'when' clause is a conditional that skips a task if the expression evaluates to false. It uses Jinja2 expressions but without {{ }}. Example: when: ansible_facts['os_family'] == 'Debian'. Multiple conditions can be combined with 'and'/'or' or as a list.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the 'state: present' parameter in ansible.builtin.package?

options.text: Upgrades the package to the latest version
Installs the package if not already installed (idempotent)
Checks if the package is installed without making changes

questions.explanation: state: present installs the package if it's not already installed. If it's already installed, no action is taken (the task reports 'ok' not 'changed'). This is idempotency — running the playbook multiple times has the same effect as running it once. Use state: latest to also upgrade.

questions.text: If two tasks notify the same handler, how many times does the handler run?

options.text: Zero times — handlers only run if explicitly called
It depends on the listen keyword

questions.explanation: Handlers are deduplicated — even if notified multiple times in a play, a handler only runs once at the end of that play. This prevents multiple service restarts when several config files change. Use 'meta: flush_handlers' if you need handlers to run immediately.

questions.text: What does 'gather_facts: false' do in a play?

options.text: Skips the automatic fact-gathering step, speeding up the play
Runs the play without privilege escalation

questions.explanation: Setting gather_facts: false skips the 'Gathering Facts' task that runs at the start of each play. This speeds up execution when you don't need system facts. Note: ansible_facts variables will be unavailable if you do this, so 'when' conditions using them will fail.

questions.text: Which flag runs a playbook in 'dry run' mode to show what would change without making changes?
questions.explanation: The --check flag runs a playbook in check mode (dry run). Ansible simulates the actions without actually making changes and reports what would have changed. Some modules support --diff to show exact file changes. Not all modules support check mode fully.
